topics: 📜 'שור או חמור' — vessels excluded from pit liability
🌬️ R' Yochanan & Reish Lakish: דרך נפילה פטרה תורה
📖 Mishnah 5:8 — 'ox' is just shorthand for any beheima
🐓 Kilayim of birds — even similar species count
🚪 Mishnah 6:1 — locking the corral and stolen flocks

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Where 'Ox' Stops and Animal Begins
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The end of perek 5 turns the spotlight on a single Torah word: 'shor.' Why does the verse say 'ox or donkey' if the rules apply to all animals? The Yerushalmi answers with a brilliant pivot: שֶׁדִּיבֵּר הַכָּתוּב בַּהוֹוֶה — Scripture spoke in the typical case. Then the daf surveys eight halakhot where 'shor' really means 'any beheima,' weighing in on kilayim of similar birds. Perek 6 then opens with the corral — when does proper locking exempt the owner from his flock's damage?
overview.hebrewSource: אִם כֵן לָמָּה נֶאֱמַר שׁוֹר אוֹ חֲמוֹר. אֶלָּא שֶׁדִּיבֵּר הַכָּתוּב בַּהוֹוֶה

slides.title: 💬 Vessels in the Pit — Three Voices
slides.subtitle: Why are vessels exempt even when the animal is liable?

lines.speaker: Logic
lines.text: If a damage-pit (less than 10 tefachim) is liable for vessels, surely a death-pit (10+) should be — kal va-chomer!

lines.speaker: Pasuk
lines.text: וְנָפַל שָׁמָּה שׁוֹר אוֹ חֲמוֹר — only ox or donkey, not 'an ox with its equipment.' The Torah blocks the kal va-chomer.

lines.speaker: Shmuel
lines.text: Vessels are only exempt when the animal got disoriented mid-air. If they hit the floor — חַיָיב.

lines.speaker: R' Y / RL
lines.text: Even if they hit the floor, פָּטוּר. דֶּרֶךְ נְפִילָה פָטְרָה תוֹרָה — the Torah exempted the entire act of falling, regardless of where the damage happens.

callout.label: Yerushalmi style:
callout.text: A clean halakhic principle (vessels are exempt) is shown to rest on two layers — the pasuk's wording and the conceptual frame of דֶּרֶךְ נְפִילָה.

slides.title: 🐂 Why Specifically 'Shor o Chamor'?
slides.subtitle: The Mishnah lists eight halakhot where 'shor' covers every animal.

boxes.title: 📜 Pit / כפל / השב אבדה
items: הָשֵׁב תְּשִׁיבֵם — return any lost animal

boxes.title: 💪 פריקה / חסימה
items: לֹא תַחְסוֹם שׁוֹר בְּדִישׁוֹ — applies to all

callout.label: The hermeneutic move:
callout.text: שֶׁדִּיבֵּר הַכָּתוּב בַּהוֹוֶה — when Torah names the most common case, the rule extends to all parallel members of the category. 'Ox' is shorthand, not exclusion.

slides.title: 🐓 Kilayim — Similar Species That Don't Count
slides.subtitle: How far does 'wild animals and birds the same' reach?

callout.label: R' Yochanan's principle:
callout.text: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁדּוֹמִין זֶה לָזֶה כִּלְאַיִם זֶה בָּזֶה — visual similarity doesn't override species distinction. Rooster + pheasant = kilayim despite resemblance.

slides.title: 📖 The Mishnah 6:1 — Locked Corrals
slides.subtitle: When does shemira shift the loss?
slides.hebrewSource: הַכּוֹנֵס צֹאן לַדִּיר וְנָעַל בְּפָנֶיהָ כָּרָאוּי וְיָצְתָה וְהִזִּיקָה חַיָיב
slides.label: Three cases:
slides.text: 1. Locked properly, animal escaped on its own — חַיָיב. 2. Wall broken at night, or robbers broke in and animal escaped — פָּטוּר. 3. Robbers actively let them out — the robbers bear liability.
slides.extraHtml: The threshold: Even 'as he should' locking isn't enough if the animal still gets out and damages — because the Torah's standard of shemira for keren is higher. Each אב has its own bar of shmira.

slides.title: 🔄 The Three-Way Argument on the Corral
slides.subtitle: Is the Mishnah following one Tanna or all?

steps.content: 1️⃣ R' La in the name of R' Yannai: this Mishnah reflects a מַחֲלוֹקֶת — only one Tanna's view.
2️⃣ R' Eleazar: דִּבְרֵי הַכֹּל — everyone holds this.
3️⃣ R' La's reasoning: shemira for the body is only written for keren; for foot/tooth, the standard differs.
4️⃣ Reish Lakish: בְּמַחֲלוֹקֶת — agreed it's contested.
5️⃣ Some say: it follows R' Eliezer ben Yaakov, who holds בֵּין כָּךְ וּבֵין כָּךְ פָּטוּר — exempt regardless.

callout.label: What's at stake:
callout.text: Whether normal locking is enough — or whether keren's higher standard requires more — depends on whose Mishnah you're reading. The Yerushalmi keeps the question alive rather than collapse it.

summary.principle: 'Shor o chamor' is a representative case, not an exclusive list — except for vessels, where the pasuk explicitly excludes them. The rules of Torah animals extend to wild beasts and birds, with kilayim sensitive even to visual look-alikes.

flowSteps.content: End of perek 5 — what does 'shor o chamor' really mean?
שֶׁדִּיבֵּר הַכָּתוּב בַּהוֹוֶה — Torah names the typical case

🎓 Key Principle:
Halakhic categories generalize from the typical, but the Torah's word choice still draws hard lines where it matters.

quiz.q: Why are vessels exempt when an animal falls into a pit and crushes them?
o: Because vessels are not valuable enough
Because the pasuk says 'shor or chamor,' not 'shor with its vessels'
quiz.e: וְנָפַל שָׁמָּה שׁוֹר אוֹ חֲמוֹר — 'shor' and not 'shor with its vessels.' The Torah blocks the otherwise compelling kal va-chomer.

quiz.q: What is R' Yochanan and Reish Lakish's principle for the falling animal's vessels?
quiz.e: Both held that the Torah exempted the entire process of falling — even if the vessels hit the ground rather than just the air.

quiz.q: Why does the Mishnah list 8 halakhot where 'shor' applies to any animal?
o: To show the Torah is repetitive
Because שֶׁדִּיבֵּר הַכָּתוּב בַּהוֹוֶה — Torah speaks in the typical case
Because each halakha was originally only for cattle
quiz.e: When the Torah names the most common animal, the rule generalizes to all members of the category.

quiz.q: According to R' Yochanan, are a rooster and a peacock-hen kilayim?
o: Yes — visual similarity doesn't override species distinction
quiz.e: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁדּוֹמִין זֶה לָזֶה כִּלְאַיִם זֶה בָּזֶה — they are kilayim despite the resemblance.

quiz.q: When robbers actively let an owner's flock out and the flock damages, who is liable?
quiz.e: הוֹצִיאוּהָ לִיסְטִין הַלִּסְטִין חַיָיבִין — the active extractors take on the liability.
